content.text: Two sentences are semantically similar if they convey the same meaning, regardless of the words used. "A dog runs in the park" and "A puppy plays outside" are very similar; "The stock market fell" is unrelated. Measuring this requires a numeric score derived from sentence embeddings — vectors that encode meaning rather than surface form.
The standard approach: encode each sentence into a fixed-size embedding vector, then compare vectors using
. Sentences with similar meaning map to nearby points in embedding space; dissimilar sentences map to far-apart points.

attrs.alt: Left: cosine geometry showing similar vs. dissimilar sentence vectors with angles. Right: score table mapping cosine values 0.97 to -0.09 to semantic relationships.

content.text: , cosine similarity measures the angle between them:
The score ranges from −1 (opposite direction) through 0 (orthogonal, no relationship) to +1 (identical direction). Most sentence transformer models
and cosine similarity reduces to a plain dot product.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")  # outputs L2-normalized vectors

sentences = [
    "A dog runs in the park",
    "A puppy plays outside",
    "The stock market fell today",
]

embeddings = model.encode(sentences, convert_to_tensor=True)

# All pairwise cosine scores (symmetric matrix)
cosine_scores = util.cos_sim(embeddings, embeddings)
print(cosine_scores)
# tensor([[1.0000, 0.9688, 0.0831],
#         [0.9688, 1.0000, 0.0742],
#         [0.0831, 0.0742, 1.0000]])

# Single pair
score = util.cos_sim(embeddings[0], embeddings[1])
print(f"Dogs vs Puppy: {score.item():.4f}")  # 0.9688
Cosine vs. Dot Product vs. Euclidean
Three distance functions are commonly used with sentence embeddings:
Default; length-invariant, works with unnormalized vectors
L2-normalized vectors (equals cosine); FAISS MIPS search
Clustering (k-means); equivalent to cosine when normalized
For L2-normalized vectors (which most sentence-transformers produce by default), cosine similarity and dot product give identical rankings. Use
Pairwise Scoring for All Sentence Pairs
When you have a small set of sentences and want all pairwise scores:

from itertools import combinations
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

sentences = [
    "How do I bake bread?",
    "Recipe for homemade sourdough",
    "The weather is nice today",
    "Best techniques for making pasta",
]

embeddings = model.encode(sentences, convert_to_tensor=True)
cosine_scores = util.cos_sim(embeddings, embeddings)  # (4, 4) matrix

# Print top pairs
pairs = []
for i, j in combinations(range(len(sentences)), 2):
    pairs.append((cosine_scores[i][j].item(), i, j))

for score, i, j in sorted(pairs, reverse=True):
    print(f"{score:.2f}  {sentences[i]!r} vs {sentences[j]!r}")
# 0.72  'How do I bake bread?' vs 'Recipe for homemade sourdough'
# 0.61  'How do I bake bread?' vs 'Best techniques for making pasta'
# 0.54  'Recipe for homemade sourdough' vs 'Best techniques for making pasta'
# 0.06  'How do I bake bread?' vs 'The weather is nice today'
: sim(A, B) = sim(B, A). This is appropriate when both sentences are of the same type (e.g., two documents, two questions).
tasks — where a short query should match a long passage — specialized models like
are trained on query/passage pairs and produce better results than symmetric models:

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

# Asymmetric model: trained for query->passage retrieval
model = SentenceTransformer("msmarco-MiniLM-L6-cos-v5")

query = "What causes thunder?"
passages = [
    "Thunder is caused by the rapid expansion of air heated by lightning.",
    "Recipes for summer salads.",
    "Lightning occurs during thunderstorms when electrical charges build up.",
]

query_emb = model.encode(query, convert_to_tensor=True)
passage_embs = model.encode(passages, convert_to_tensor=True)

scores = util.cos_sim(query_emb, passage_embs)[0]
for score, passage in sorted(zip(scores, passages), reverse=True):
    print(f"{score:.3f}  {passage[:60]}")
# 0.851  Thunder is caused by the rapid expansion of air heated...
# 0.712  Lightning occurs during thunderstorms when electrical...
# 0.031  Recipes for summer salads.
The Semantic Textual Similarity Benchmark (STS-B) is the standard evaluation dataset. Human annotators rated sentence pairs on a 0–5 scale. Models are evaluated by
between predicted cosine scores and human ratings:

from sentence_transformers.evaluation import EmbeddingSimilarityEvaluator
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from datasets import load_dataset

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
ds = load_dataset("mteb/stsbenchmark-sts", split="test")

evaluator = EmbeddingSimilarityEvaluator(
    sentences1=ds["sentence1"],
    sentences2=ds["sentence2"],
    scores=[s / 5.0 for s in ds["score"]],  # normalize to [0, 1]
)
result = evaluator(model)
print(result)  # e.g. {"spearman_cosine": 0.8816}
Cosine scores do not have universal meaning — a 0.7 from one model is not the same as 0.7 from another. Always calibrate thresholds on labeled samples from your domain:

from sklearn.metrics import f1_score
import numpy as np

# labeled_pairs: list of (sentence1, sentence2, label) where label ∈ {0, 1}
sentences1 = [p[0] for p in labeled_pairs]
sentences2 = [p[1] for p in labeled_pairs]
labels = np.array([p[2] for p in labeled_pairs])

emb1 = model.encode(sentences1, convert_to_tensor=True)
emb2 = model.encode(sentences2, convert_to_tensor=True)
scores = util.cos_sim(emb1, emb2).diagonal().cpu().numpy()

# Find threshold that maximizes F1
best_t, best_f1 = 0.5, 0.0
for t in np.arange(0.3, 0.95, 0.05):
    preds = (scores >= t).astype(int)
    f1 = f1_score(labels, preds)
    if f1 > best_f1:
        best_t, best_f1 = t, f1

print(f"Best threshold: {best_t:.2f}  F1: {best_f1:.4f}")
to guarantee unit norm. Mixing normalized and unnormalized vectors in the same index produces wrong scores.
Encoding one sentence at a time is 10–100x slower than batching. Always pass a list and set
work well for everyday text but may underperform on legal, medical, or code corpora. Use a domain-specific model or fine-tune.
Some models produce embeddings clustered in a narrow cone, inflating all cosine scores. Apply whitening or check the distribution before setting thresholds.

Keyword search breaks on synonymy and paraphrase. If a user searches 'ways to lose weight' and your document discusses 'strategies for fat reduction', a BM25 index returns nothing because no words overlap. Semantic similarity solves this by comparing meaning rather than surface form. The query and document embed to nearby points in the same vector space, and cosine similarity captures their conceptual proximity regardless of word choice.

The economic impact is substantial. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems rely entirely on semantic similarity to find the relevant context passages that the LLM then uses to answer questions. A 10% improvement in recall@10 for a RAG system translates directly into 10% fewer hallucinated answers. For a medical RAG system serving clinicians, this difference can affect patient outcomes. For an e-commerce search engine, it translates into higher conversion rates.
Multilingual models (e.g., paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2) embed text from 50+ languages into the same space, enabling cross-lingual retrieval without translation.
'car' and 'automobile' embed close together; keyword search treats them as distinct.
Encoding a query takes 5–20 ms; ANN search over millions of documents takes 1–5 ms with HNSW indices.
Similarity scores from multiple embedding models can be combined with BM25 scores (hybrid retrieval) for better coverage than either alone.
Documents with cosine similarity > 0.95 are near-duplicates and can be merged. Clusters of documents with similarity > 0.7 represent topically related groups.
Encoding a corpus of 1 million documents at 100 tokens each takes roughly 1–2 hours on a single GPU. Re-encoding after model updates requires re-indexing the entire corpus.

Cosine similarity captures topic overlap, not sentiment. 'The hotel was wonderful' and 'The hotel was terrible' embed close together because they are about the same topic. Use sentiment-aware models for opinion retrieval.
A threshold calibrated for one model produces wrong decisions with a different model. Always re-calibrate after any model change.

Some models produce embeddings clustered in a narrow cone (low isotropy), inflating all cosine scores and making discrimination hard. A few 'hub' vectors become nearest neighbours of nearly every query.
Most sentence transformers are optimised for inputs up to 256–512 tokens. Long documents must be chunked; the similarity of the full document is approximated by the best-matching chunk.

The key challenge in learning sentence embeddings for semantic similarity is the lack of a training signal in raw text. Two sentences may be lexically identical but semantically different, or semantically identical but lexically different. Sentence-BERT (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019) solved this by fine-tuning BERT on NLI (Natural Language Inference) datasets using a Siamese network architecture with a contrastive objective.

The MNRL (Multiple Negatives Ranking Loss) training objective is now standard. For a batch of (query, positive) pairs, each query is trained to rank its positive passage above all other passages in the batch, which serve as in-batch negatives:
is cosine similarity, and the sum in the denominator runs over all N passages in the batch (including the true positive

plus N-1 in-batch negatives). With a batch size of 256, each query has 255 negatives, creating a very informative training signal per step. This is why large-batch training with MNRL is a strong substitute for explicit hard negative mining.
The cosine similarity between two unit-norm vectors u and v is computed as their dot product, which is the Euclidean inner product under the constraint that both vectors lie on the unit hypersphere:

Use semantic similarity when the retrieval task involves paraphrase, synonym handling, or cross-lingual content. Do not use it as a substitute for structured queries: finding all documents with a specific author, date, or exact keyword is better done with a database filter applied before or after embedding retrieval.
RAG context retrieval, semantic deduplication, question matching in FAQ systems, document clustering, and plagiarism detection.
exact keyword queries, searching by product code or entity name, or when users expect exact-match behaviour.
production search systems where robustness matters. BM25 handles exact matches; embeddings handle paraphrase and intent.
How to Implement: Semantic Search from Scratch
The following implements a full semantic search pipeline: encode a corpus, build an HNSW index with FAISS, and serve queries.

# Install: pip install sentence-transformers faiss-cpu numpy

import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import faiss

# Step 1: Encode corpus
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

corpus = [
    "How do I reset my password?",
    "What is your refund policy?",
    "My order hasn't arrived yet, what do I do?",
    "How long does shipping take?",
    "Can I change my delivery address after placing an order?",
    "How do I return a damaged item?",
    "What payment methods do you accept?",
    "How do I track my package?",
    "I need to cancel my subscription.",
    "Where is my invoice?",
]

print("Encoding corpus...")
corpus_embeddings = model.encode(
    corpus,
    normalize_embeddings=True,   # L2-normalize for cosine = dot product
    batch_size=32,
    show_progress_bar=False,
)
corpus_embeddings = corpus_embeddings.astype(np.float32)

# Step 2: Build HNSW index (approximate nearest neighbour)
dim = corpus_embeddings.shape[1]  # 384 for all-MiniLM-L6-v2
index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(dim, 32)   # 32 = HNSW M parameter
index.add(corpus_embeddings)
print(f"Index built: {index.ntotal} vectors, dim={dim}")

# Step 3: Query
def semantic_search(query: str, top_k: int = 3) -> list[dict]:
    q_emb = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
    scores, indices = index.search(q_emb, top_k)

    results = []
    for score, idx in zip(scores[0], indices[0]):
        results.append({
            "score": float(score),
            "text": corpus[idx],
        })
    return results


queries = [
    "I forgot my login credentials",
    "package tracking information",
    "want to send the product back",
]

for q in queries:
    print(f"\nQuery: {q!r}")
    for r in semantic_search(q, top_k=2):
        print(f"  {r['score']:.3f}  {r['text']}")
# Query: 'I forgot my login credentials'
#   0.742  How do I reset my password?
#   0.481  What payment methods do you accept?
# Query: 'package tracking information'
#   0.821  How do I track my package?
#   0.673  My order hasn't arrived yet, what do I do?

A content platform receives thousands of user-submitted articles daily. Many are near-duplicates: the same story submitted twice, or two articles that cover the same event with different wording. Semantic deduplication clusters near-duplicates so editors see only representative originals.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

articles = [
    "Apple announces new iPhone with improved camera and longer battery life",
    "Apple unveils latest iPhone featuring enhanced photography and extended battery",
    "Google releases Pixel phone with advanced AI camera features",
    "New iPhone from Apple boasts better photos and battery performance",
    "Google's new Pixel smartphone uses AI for superior camera quality",
    "Tesla reports record quarterly revenue amid rising EV demand",
]

embeddings = model.encode(articles, normalize_embeddings=True, convert_to_tensor=True)
cos_sim = util.cos_sim(embeddings, embeddings).cpu().numpy()

DEDUP_THRESHOLD = 0.85  # sentences above this are near-duplicates

# Greedy clustering: assign each article to the first cluster it's similar to
clusters: list[list[int]] = []
assigned = set()

for i in range(len(articles)):
    if i in assigned:
        continue
    cluster = [i]
    assigned.add(i)
    for j in range(i + 1, len(articles)):
        if j not in assigned and cos_sim[i, j] >= DEDUP_THRESHOLD:
            cluster.append(j)
            assigned.add(j)
    clusters.append(cluster)

print(f"Found {len(clusters)} unique clusters from {len(articles)} articles:")
for c_idx, cluster in enumerate(clusters):
    print(f"\nCluster {c_idx + 1}:")
    for art_idx in cluster:
        marker = "[REPRESENTATIVE]" if art_idx == cluster[0] else "  [DUPLICATE]  "
        print(f"  {marker} {articles[art_idx]}")

# Cluster 1:
#   [REPRESENTATIVE] Apple announces new iPhone with improved camera...
#     [DUPLICATE]   Apple unveils latest iPhone featuring enhanced photography...
#     [DUPLICATE]   New iPhone from Apple boasts better photos...
# Cluster 2:
#   [REPRESENTATIVE] Google releases Pixel phone with advanced AI camera features
#     [DUPLICATE]   Google's new Pixel smartphone uses AI for superior camera quality
# Cluster 3:
#   [REPRESENTATIVE] Tesla reports record quarterly revenue amid rising EV demand
Mixing normalised and unnormalised embeddings in the same index.
If you encode your corpus with normalize_embeddings=True but encode queries without, dot products will not equal cosine similarity and scores will be wrong.
Using a symmetric model for query-passage retrieval.
Models like all-MiniLM-L6-v2 are trained on symmetric pairs. For query→passage retrieval, use an asymmetric model like msmarco-MiniLM-L6-cos-v5 trained on MSMARCO.
Using a fixed threshold without calibration.
A threshold of 0.7 that works for one model and domain may produce 80% false positives or 80% false negatives for a different model. Always calibrate on labelled data from your domain.
Encoding very long documents as a single vector.
Most sentence transformers truncate at 512 tokens. A 5,000-word document encoded as one vector captures only the first 512 tokens. Chunk documents and embed each chunk separately.
Encoding sentences one at a time is 10–100x slower than batching. Always pass a list and let the model encode in batches.
when you intend to use cosine or dot product similarity. This makes cosine = dot product and enables FAISS IndexFlatIP for exact search.
Use HNSW for ANN search over large corpora
(> 100K documents). HNSW achieves 95–99% recall at 10–100x the speed of exact search.
Chunk long documents at sentence or paragraph boundaries
, not at fixed token counts. Mid-sentence truncation produces worse embeddings than complete-sentence chunking.
from sentence-transformers to measure Spearman ρ on your domain data before and after any model change.
If your corpus rarely changes, store embeddings to disk (np.save) and reload them instead of re-encoding on each startup.

Semantic similarity at scale has two phases: offline indexing (encode all documents) and online retrieval (encode query + nearest-neighbour search). Offline indexing throughput: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 encodes roughly 4,000–6,000 sentences/second on a single A100 with batch_size=256. A 1-million-document corpus (100 tokens/doc average) takes roughly 3–5 minutes. Larger models (768-dim, e.g., all-mpnet-base-v2) encode at 1,500–2,000 sentences/second.

Online retrieval latency is dominated by the ANN search, not the encoding. HNSW search over 1 million 384-dim vectors takes 0.5–2 ms on CPU with FAISS; query encoding takes 5–15 ms on CPU or 1–3 ms on GPU. Total query-to-results latency for a typical semantic search pipeline is 10–30 ms on CPU-only infrastructure.

Memory footprint: each 384-dim float32 embedding takes 1.5 KB. 1 million embeddings require 1.5 GB of RAM (or 750 MB in float16). The HNSW index structure adds 1–2x overhead. For corpora over 10 million documents, use Product Quantisation (FAISS IVF-PQ) to compress to 50–100 bytes per vector (30–50x compression) with a ~5% recall trade-off.
Flat, HNSW, IVF-PQ indices; GPU support
Massive Text Embedding Benchmark — 56 tasks across 112 languages
Standard semantic similarity benchmark, human-rated pairs
Orchestrates embeddings, retrieval, and LLM generation

Semantic search exposes attack surface that keyword search does not. Adversarial embedding injection — crafting a document that embeds very close to many unrelated queries — can cause a retrieval system to return attacker-controlled content regardless of what the user searched for. This is particularly dangerous in RAG systems where retrieved content is fed to an LLM and can influence its response.

Mitigations: (1) filter retrieved documents against a confidence threshold — do not retrieve a document unless its cosine similarity to the query exceeds a minimum (e.g., 0.5); (2) apply post-retrieval reranking with a cross-encoder that jointly scores query and document, which is harder to fool than bi-encoder retrieval; (3) never concatenate retrieved content directly into the LLM prompt without sanitisation.

For reliability, monitor recall@k on a held-out test set weekly. Embedding models degrade relative to the corpus as the domain evolves. A model trained before 2023 will embed recent LLM terminology ("chain-of-thought", "RLHF", "RAG") suboptimally. Set up automated alerts when recall@10 drops below a threshold, triggering a model re-evaluation or retraining.
Semantic similarity measures meaning overlap, not surface form — enabling retrieval that is robust to paraphrase, synonymy, and cross-lingual content.
Cosine similarity between unit-norm embeddings equals their dot product, making exact cosine search equivalent to maximum inner product search.
Symmetric models (all-MiniLM) are best for sentence-pair tasks; asymmetric models (msmarco-MiniLM) are better for query-to-passage retrieval.
Thresholds are model- and domain-specific — always calibrate on labelled data from your application before deploying.
Hybrid retrieval (BM25 + semantic embeddings) consistently outperforms either method alone; use Reciprocal Rank Fusion to combine scores.
For large corpora, use FAISS HNSW for approximate search (0.5–2 ms per query) rather than exact search (which scales linearly with corpus size).
Apply post-retrieval cross-encoder reranking for both quality improvement and as a defence against embedding injection attacks.
